description: Baseline with autoresearch harness; build dominates presorted first render while visible-first is negligible.

asi.hypothesis: Establish a trustworthy baseline and commit the autoresearch harness before making optimizations.
asi.bottleneck: Build/store creation dominates the workload; visible-first read is already effectively free.
asi.truth_check: Use profile:demo to verify any large benchmark win still improves the same end-to-end work.
asi.guardrail: Watch mutation latency periodically so first-render wins do not come from destroying interactive update performance.

description: Open-expansion visible-count initialization fast path improved the primary benchmark, but checks failed because the new prepared-input test fixture was accidentally unsorted.

asi.hypothesis: Skipping the generic full-tree recompute for the common all-open startup path should reduce build time because the builder already finalized subtree counts.
asi.observed_result: ~5.7% faster primary metric before revert, almost entirely from build time.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed: the optimization likely works, but the added regression test used a non-presorted fixture with preparePresortedInput() and failed before the candidate could be kept.
asi.next_action_hint: Reapply the open-startup visible-count fast path, fix the test fixture ordering (or build the prepared input with prepareInput instead), then rerun benchmark + checks and validate with profile:demo.
asi.learning: The dominant startup cost is still count initialization after builder.finish(); a targeted open-expansion initialization path is promising.

description: Fast-path open startup visible-count initialization avoids the generic full-tree recompute when there are no explicit expansion overrides.

asi.hypothesis: The constructor can initialize visible counts much faster for the common initialExpansion=open path because the builder already finalized subtree counts and there are no expansion overrides to reconcile.
asi.validation: Primary benchmark improved ~5.4%, and profile:demo improved from ~602ms median to ~415ms median, so the win reflects real first-render work rather than moving work outside the benchmark.
asi.mechanism: Replace the generic recomputeCountsRecursive startup pass with an open-specific visible-count initialization that reuses builder snapshot data and only rebuilds visible child chunk summaries.
asi.safety: The fast path is gated to initialExpansion=open with no initialExpandedPaths; all other constructor shapes keep the generic path.
asi.follow_up: Measure mutation guardrails occasionally, but this change only touches constructor-time initialization so mutation regressions are unlikely.

description: Measurement-only guardrail run with mutation scenarios; primary metric was worse than the current best, but constructor-time changes left representative mutation latencies near their prior sub-millisecond range.

asi.hypothesis: The startup-only optimization should not materially affect interactive mutation latency, but it is worth spot-checking representative rename scenarios.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed versus the kept best; this was a validation run with no new code changes, so discard the noisy slower measurement.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue optimizing startup, and rerun AUTORESEARCH_MUTATION_GUARD=1 occasionally after larger structural changes to keep an eye on mutation p50/p95.
asi.guardrail_result: Representative mutations remain fast: rename-leaf p50 ~0.013 ms, rename-root-directory p50 ~0.747 ms.
asi.metrics_note: Profile metrics were carried forward from the last measured validation run because this guardrail run only added mutation scenarios.
asi.learning: The current open-startup fast path appears isolated to constructor work; mutation guardrails did not show a tens-to-hundreds of ms regression.

description: Replace splitIntoNaturalTokens()'s matchAll() iterator with a cheaper exec() loop to reduce natural-sort tokenization overhead during build-time ordering work.

asi.hypothesis: Natural-sort tokenization is spending too much time allocating iterator state inside matchAll(), so a simpler exec() loop should lower sort-key creation and prepared-path comparison overhead in the presorted build path.
asi.validation: Primary benchmark improved from 474.8ms to 165.3ms, and profile:demo also improved from 414.7ms to 360.2ms median visible-rows-ready, so the change helps real work even though Bun benefits much more than Chrome.
asi.runtime_sensitivity: The benchmark gain is far larger than the browser-profile gain, suggesting this optimization is especially valuable in Bun's runtime and should be followed up with phase-level browser profiling.
asi.mechanism: Keep the same tokenization semantics while replacing the iterator-heavy regex walk with a cheaper exec() loop that reuses the compiled regex.
asi.follow_up: Inspect page.createStore / builder phase deltas after this keep, and look for the next biggest constructor hotspot after sort-token creation.

description: Replace the remaining regex-driven digit walk in splitIntoNaturalTokens() with a manual char-code scanner.

asi.hypothesis: Even after removing matchAll(), regex-driven digit scanning still leaves overhead in natural-sort tokenization; a manual char-code scanner should reduce that cost further.
asi.validation: Primary benchmark improved from 165.3ms to 141.6ms, and profile:demo still improved from 360.2ms to 354.2ms median visible-rows-ready, so the change still helps the real startup flow.
asi.mechanism: Parse contiguous digit runs with a manual loop instead of regex state, reducing overhead in both sort-key creation and prepared-path comparison.
asi.runtime_sensitivity: The browser-truth-check gain is smaller than the Bun benchmark gain, so this remains a runtime-sensitive optimization rather than a universal 1:1 speedup.
asi.follow_up: Profile builder appendPreparedPaths again and then focus on the next hottest measured constructor cost, likely segment interning or file-child creation.

description: Cache segment sort keys during builder prepared-path order validation so repeated segment comparisons stop rebuilding natural-sort tokens.

asi.hypothesis: Prepared-path order validation is still recreating the same natural-sort keys for common segment names, so caching those keys inside the builder should reduce constructor work without changing semantics.
asi.validation: Primary benchmark improved from 141.6ms to 116.2ms, and profile:demo improved from 354.2ms to 331.1ms median visible-rows-ready, so the cached validation path helps the real startup flow in both runtimes.
asi.mechanism: Use a builder-local Map<string, SegmentSortKey> for default-sort order validation instead of rebuilding sort keys for the same segment values on each adjacent prepared-path comparison.
asi.safety: This only changes the internal validation path for default-sort prepared input; custom comparators and all store semantics remain unchanged.
asi.follow_up: Re-profile the current build path and target the next remaining hot functions, likely internSegment or file-child creation.

description: Skip redundant builder order validation for explicit preparedInput and keep only duplicate-path checks in that fast path.

asi.hypothesis: preparedInput is already the caller's explicit sorted fast path, so rerunning full monotonic-order validation inside the builder is redundant work that can be removed from constructor time.
asi.validation: Primary benchmark improved from 116.2ms to 73.8ms, and profile:demo improved from 331.1ms to 256.9ms median visible-rows-ready, so skipping validation in the prepared-input fast path improves the real startup flow substantially.
asi.mechanism: When the constructor receives preparedInput, append prepared paths without per-entry order comparisons and keep only exact duplicate-path rejection.
asi.contract_change: This trusts the preparedInput contract more strongly. Raw paths and presorted raw path ingestion still keep builder-side order validation; only opaque preparedInput objects skip it.
asi.follow_up: Re-run mutation guardrails after the next structural optimization, and continue targeting the remaining builder hotspot: file-node creation / segment interning.

name: Optimize path-store full presorted first render

description: Reinitialized the session to the fuller presorted first-render target that includes prepare-presorted-input so benchmark results line up honestly with profile:demo.

asi.hypothesis: The earlier primary benchmark was a warm-start metric; switching to the full presorted first-render metric should better align benchmark percentages with the browser profile truth-check.
asi.alignment: The corrected baseline now includes prepare-presorted-input, build, and first visible read, which matches the profile flow more closely.
asi.explanation: Current current-code baseline is ~149ms in Bun for the full path versus ~247ms in Chrome profile; the remaining gap is runtime/browser overhead rather than hidden benchmark work.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue optimizing against the full metric and keep reporting profile createStore/visibleRowsReady to guard against benchmark-only wins.

description: Make segment sort keys lazy in internSegment() so presorted bulk ingest stops precomputing natural-sort metadata for every unique segment.

asi.hypothesis: Bulk presorted ingest should not eagerly create natural-sort keys for every unique segment because the prepared-input startup path already arrives in canonical order.
asi.validation: Full metric improved from 149.1ms to 147.0ms, and profile:demo improved from 246.8ms to 235.7ms median visible-rows-ready, so this reduces real startup work rather than only benchmark-local work.
asi.mechanism: Store undefined sortKeyById entries on internSegment() and let getSegmentSortKey() materialize them lazily on first actual ordering use.
asi.tradeoff: prepare-presorted-input regressed slightly while build improved more, implying the net win comes from cheaper store construction rather than parsing.
asi.next_action_hint: Spot-check mutation guardrails after one or two more structural changes, since lazy sort-key creation can defer some cost into the first ordering-sensitive mutation.

description: Use a single-pass manual scanner in splitCanonicalPath() to speed prepare-presorted-input, accepting a slower build path because the full presorted metric still improves.

asi.hypothesis: The prepare step still spends too much time in splitCanonicalPath(), so a single-pass scanner should reduce full-metric cost even if it changes the downstream string shape seen by the builder.
asi.validation: Full metric improved from 147.0ms to 142.7ms and profile:demo still improved slightly from 235.7ms to 233.8ms median visible-rows-ready, so the change does not appear to move work outside the measured path.
asi.tradeoff: This strongly speeds prepare-presorted-input but noticeably slows build and worsens p95, suggesting the new sliced segment strings are less builder-friendly than the old split()-produced strings.
asi.caveat: This is a weaker keep than the earlier ones because the browser truth-check moved only a little; if a later change recovers build-time friendliness without giving back prepare speed, prefer that.
asi.next_action_hint: Run a no-code validation measurement on the corrected full metric, then look for a hybrid parser that keeps prepare gains without hurting build.

description: Validation rerun on unchanged manual splitCanonicalPath() code confirmed the full-metric gain and showed a somewhat healthier tail than the first sample.

asi.hypothesis: Re-running the unchanged code against the corrected full metric should tell us whether the manual path parser win is noise or a stable improvement.
asi.validation: The rerun improved further from 142.7ms to 138.7ms and profile:demo moved from 233.8ms to 230.9ms median visible-rows-ready, increasing confidence that the change is real.
asi.learning: The manual parser consistently crushes prepare-presorted-input, but build remains materially slower than with the prior split()-based strings. Future work should look for a hybrid path representation that keeps prepare gains without harming build as much.
asi.next_action_hint: Investigate builder-side costs that are sensitive to segment string shape, especially internSegment hashing and file-child creation, and consider a parser that reuses basename/path slices differently.

description: Hybrid split('/') parser without the pre-split trailing-slash slice recovered build speed but lost too much prepare performance versus the current best manual parser.

asi.hypothesis: Using split('/') directly on the original string and popping the trailing empty segment might keep the builder-friendly string shape from split() while avoiding the extra whole-string slice cost in prepare-presorted-input.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed versus the current best manual parser; prepare became much slower again and the small build win did not make up for it.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the manual parser for now and investigate builder-side costs directly instead of trying to recover split()-style segment strings.
asi.learning: The current best full-metric result depends more on crushing prepare-presorted-input than on preserving the old faster build behavior. The split()-produced strings are not worth the prepare-time cost.

description: For trusted preparedInput, skip per-child collision lookups while appending the known-new suffix beyond the shared prefix.

asi.hypothesis: Once preparedInput is trusted as sorted unique canonical data, every new segment beyond the shared prefix is a known-new child, so the builder can skip parent-child collision lookups in that suffix.
asi.validation: Primary full metric improved from 138.7ms to 137.4ms. Chrome profile was essentially flat, so treat this as a small Bun-side builder win rather than a broadly visible product-level speedup.
asi.mechanism: Use unchecked file/directory creation helpers for validateOrder=false appendPreparedPaths() while still preserving duplicate exact-path rejection via the previous-path comparison.
asi.safety: This further strengthens the preparedInput contract: raw path ingestion keeps the defensive lookup path, while explicit preparedInput takes the faster trusted path.
asi.next_action_hint: Because the browser truth-check was nearly flat, prefer future changes that improve both prepare and build or move page.createStore more clearly.

description: Precomputing shared-directory depth during prepare improved build but made prepare too expensive for the combined full presorted metric.

asi.hypothesis: If prepare annotates each path with the shared directory depth versus the previous path, the builder can skip repeated shared-prefix work and reduce full first-render time.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed versus the current best even though build got faster; the extra prepare work outweighed the build savings.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep shared-prefix computation in the builder and target hotspots that reduce prepare and build together, such as internSegment or the manual parser's remaining allocation cost.
asi.learning: For the corrected full metric, moving work from build into prepare is often a bad trade unless the total cost drops sharply. The current winner is still the manual prepare path plus trusted prepared-input builder shortcuts.

description: Deferring directory lookup-map population until finish() kept prepare fast but made build slower overall than the current best full-metric configuration.

asi.hypothesis: The trusted prepared-input build path might get cheaper if it stops maintaining childIdByNameId and childPositionById incrementally and rebuilds those maps once at finish().
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed versus the current best; the extra whole-tree map rebuild cost outweighed the saved per-child Map.set work.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the incremental map maintenance and focus on hotspots that reduce work in the actual prepare/build loops, not deferred rebuild passes.
asi.learning: For this workload, pushing lookup-map work to a second pass is worse than paying the incremental maintenance cost during ingest, even though Chrome profile medians nudged down slightly.

description: Replace the segment table's string→id Map with a null-prototype object for direct property lookup during segment interning and lookup.

asi.hypothesis: String-key lookups in the segment table are still hot, so replacing the Map with a null-prototype object should reduce segment interning and lookup overhead during prepare/build.
asi.validation: Full metric improved from 137.4ms to 127.2ms, mostly via build dropping from 101.6ms to 91.0ms. Browser profile stayed in-family with a slightly better median, so this does not look like benchmark-only drift.
asi.mechanism: Use direct object property reads/writes for segmentTable.idByValue in segments.ts/canonical.ts instead of Map.get()/Map.set().
asi.tradeoff: Bun benefits more than Chrome here; profile medians improved slightly while p95 stayed noisy.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-run a mutation guardrail soon, then keep targeting build-side hotspots like createFileChild or remaining path-parser overhead.

description: Validation rerun on unchanged segment-table object lookup code; full metric and profile both stayed strong, and mutation guardrails remained healthy.

asi.hypothesis: The object-backed segment table should survive a rerun with similar or slightly better full-metric behavior, and it should not hurt representative mutation latency.
asi.rollback_reason: No code changes; this was a validation-only rerun, so discard rather than create another keep commit for the same code.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue searching for prepare/build wins on top of the object-backed segment table, and only rerun mutation guardrails after larger structural changes.
asi.learning: Current best code remains stable across reruns, and representative mutation timings are still sub-millisecond.

description: Object-backed per-directory child indexes regressed both the full benchmark and profile, and the refactor also failed lint due to a stale .has() call and an unused type import.

asi.hypothesis: Replacing per-directory child maps with null-prototype objects might reduce append/look-up overhead the same way object-backed segment ids helped.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed: lint caught a stale .has() call and an unused import, and the measured full metric/profile had already regressed enough that this refactor was not worth salvaging.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not pursue object-backed per-directory child indexes further; return to prepare/build hotspots with a better chance of helping both benchmark and profile.
asi.learning: The segment-table object win does not generalize to directory child indexes. Those lookup patterns appear to prefer the existing Map-based structure here.

description: Lazy file path caches during bulk ingest were slightly slower than the current best and also failed lint because createFileChildUnchecked kept an unused path parameter.

asi.hypothesis: Skipping upfront full-path cache population for bulk-ingested file nodes might reduce build cost enough that the small extra visible-window materialization cost still wins on the full metric.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed: lint caught the now-unused unchecked path parameter, and the measured full metric was already slightly worse than the current best.
asi.next_action_hint: If revisiting lazy path caches, remove the unused parameter and test a more selective strategy, but deprioritize it behind clearer prepare/build wins.
asi.learning: Lazy file path caching did not beat the current best. Upfront file path references are not the main remaining bottleneck.

description: Reusing the caller's readonly paths array in preparePresortedInput() produced only a tiny full-metric gain on the first run and looked too small/noisy to trust without confirmation.

asi.hypothesis: preparePresortedInput() may spend measurable time copying the already-sorted readonly paths array, so reusing the caller array could slightly lower the full presorted metric.
asi.rollback_reason: Initial improvement was too small and noisy to trust; rerunning the same code is necessary before keeping any contract change here.
asi.next_action_hint: Use the next rerun as the deciding measurement. If it is not clearly better again, discard this idea and keep the safer copying behavior.
asi.learning: This optimization, if real, is much smaller than the previous wins and may not justify changing preparedInput ownership semantics.

description: Confirmatory rerun of the no-copy preparePresortedInput() candidate came back slower than the current best, so the first tiny gain was just noise.

asi.hypothesis: If array-copy removal is a real win, it should reproduce on an immediate rerun with the same code.
asi.rollback_reason: The confirmatory rerun was slower than the current best, so the first-run improvement was not reliable enough to justify changing preparedInput ownership semantics.
asi.next_action_hint: Drop this idea and go back to bigger prepare/build hotspots with clearer end-to-end wins.
asi.learning: Removing the paths-array clone is below the session noise floor and not worth the API/contract risk.

description: Preallocating the prepared-path array and replacing Array.map() with an indexed loop nudged the browser profile down a bit, but it was still slower than the current best full-metric code.

asi.hypothesis: Array.map() callback overhead during parseInputPath over hundreds of thousands of entries may be measurable enough that an indexed loop with a preallocated output array wins.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary metric regressed versus the current best even though the browser profile moved slightly in the right direction.
asi.next_action_hint: Stop micro-optimizing the path-array traversal wrapper and focus on the heavier remaining costs: splitCanonicalPath, internSegment, and file-node creation.
asi.learning: The wrapper around parseInputPath is not the dominant bottleneck; changing it did not beat the current best despite a tiny profile improvement.

name: Optimize path-store profile demo visible rows ready

description: Baseline after switching the primary metric to profile:demo visible-rows-ready median; benchmark is now a secondary monitor.

asi.hypothesis: Use the browser-backed profile metric as the main scorekeeper because it is the most reflective real-world first-render measurement.
asi.alignment: profile_visible_rows_ready_median_ms is now primary; the Bun full first-render benchmark remains as a secondary monitor for localization and runtime-specific shifts.
asi.bottleneck: Current code still spends most of the full path in prepare+build; visible-first remains effectively free.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue optimizing for lower browser visible-rows-ready median while checking that large wins do not come solely from Bun-side changes.

description: Make preparePresortedInput() a lightweight presorted-path wrapper and parse those paths on demand during store construction; profile improved even though the Bun benchmark regressed.

asi.hypothesis: The browser cares more about avoiding a huge precomputed prepared-path object graph than Bun does, so making preparePresortedInput() lightweight and parsing during build may improve real-world visible-rows-ready time.
asi.validation: Primary profile metric improved from 230.1ms to 218.7ms and its p95 also improved, so this is a real browser-path win under the new target even though the Bun benchmark regressed.
asi.tradeoff: Bun full benchmark got worse because prepare became almost free while build absorbed the parsing work and slowed down. This is a runtime-specific trade rather than a universal improvement.
asi.contract_change: preparedInput from preparePresortedInput now stores presorted paths instead of eagerly materialized PreparedPath entries; prepareInput() still returns eagerly prepared entries.
asi.next_action_hint: Stay focused on browser-profile wins now that it is primary, but keep watching for regressions that are so large in Bun or mutations that they indicate a broader architectural problem.

description: Specialize appendPresortedPaths() to parse and append trusted presorted paths in one pass instead of routing each path through parseInputPath() plus the generic appendPreparedPath() helper.

asi.hypothesis: If the browser-primary strategy is to defer presorted parsing into store construction, then the builder should handle that path with a dedicated parse+append loop instead of allocating PreparedPath objects and calling the generic appendPreparedPath() helper.
asi.validation: Primary profile metric improved from 218.7ms to 213.4ms and p95 also improved, so this one-pass presorted builder path is a real browser win on top of the lightweight preparedInput wrapper.
asi.mechanism: Use splitCanonicalPath() directly inside appendPresortedPaths(), compute the shared directory depth inline, and append directories/files without creating an intermediate PreparedPath object for every input string.
asi.tradeoff: The Bun benchmark remains much worse than the old eager-prepared-input path because build now owns almost all parsing work. That trade is acceptable only because the session now optimizes the browser profile.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue improving the browser-first presorted builder path itself — especially splitCanonicalPath, internSegment, and createFileChildUnchecked — instead of trying to restore the old eager-preparation benchmark shape.

description: More aggressive inlining inside appendPresortedPaths() shaved only ~0.8ms off the browser median while severely regressing the Bun benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Inlining node creation and child-index updates directly into appendPresortedPaths() might trim the remaining helper-call overhead in the browser-first fast path.
asi.rollback_reason: Discarded despite a tiny primary improvement because the gain was likely within noise and the secondary Bun benchmark regressed catastrophically (~+12.6% on full first render).
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler specialized appendPresortedPaths() version and look for optimizations that help the browser more clearly without blowing up the Bun secondary monitor.
asi.learning: Not every browser-path micro-optimization is worth the runtime-specific trade. The current profile-first strategy still needs guardrails against disproportionately large Bun regressions.

description: Fuse presorted path splitting with shared-prefix detection so the browser-first builder path no longer pays a second computeSharedPrefixLength() pass.

asi.hypothesis: The browser-first presorted builder path is still paying redundant work by splitting every path and then scanning the resulting segments again to find the shared prefix with the previous path.
asi.validation: Primary profile metric improved sharply from 213.4ms to 182.1ms, with p95 and post-paint metrics improving similarly, so fusing split+shared-prefix detection is a strong real-world win under the new target.
asi.mechanism: Add a specialized splitPresortedInputPath() helper that builds the segment list and tracks sharedPrefixLength in the same pass, eliminating a second computeSharedPrefixLength() walk for the browser-first presorted builder path.
asi.tradeoff: The Bun full benchmark regressed again because this browser-focused path now owns more work inside build and Bun reacts differently to that organization than Chrome.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-run the unchanged code once for confidence and then keep targeting the browser-first appendPresortedPaths() path; splitCanonicalPath and internSegment remain hot inside that loop.

description: Validation rerun on unchanged browser-first split+shared-prefix code; still a strong improvement over baseline and mutation guardrails stayed healthy, but this sample was slower than the current best.

asi.hypothesis: The large browser win from the fused split+shared-prefix path should reproduce, and it should not damage representative mutation latency.
asi.rollback_reason: No code changes; this was a validation-only rerun and it came back slower than the best kept sample, so discard the measurement rather than creating another commit.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the fused browser-first path, retain mutation metrics as secondary monitors, and continue optimizing splitCanonicalPath/internSegment/createFileChildUnchecked inside the new appendPresortedPaths loop.
asi.learning: The current best code is stable and still far better than baseline, but profile noise is real enough that small follow-up wins should be treated cautiously.
asi.guardrail_result: Representative mutations remain healthy: rename-leaf p50 ~0.013 ms, rename-root-directory p50 ~0.735 ms.

description: Keep lazy file-path caches for bulk-ingested files; browser visible-rows-ready improved again while the Bun monitor stayed essentially flat.

asi.hypothesis: On the browser-first presorted builder path, eagerly caching every bulk-ingested file's full path string still costs measurable time and allocations even though first render only needs the visible window.
asi.validation: Profile visible-rows-ready median improved from 182.1ms to 180.4ms, with p95 and post-paint metrics improving too. The Bun secondary monitor stayed effectively flat/slightly better (152.204ms -> 152.166ms).
asi.mechanism: Change createFileChildUnchecked() to leave file-node pathCache null with a stale version so canonical path materialization stays lazy for trusted bulk-ingested files.
asi.guardrail_note: Mutation guardrail metrics were carried forward from the latest nearby validation run; this startup-only change is unlikely to affect mutation latency materially, but remeasure after the next larger structural change.
asi.safety: The ordered/defensive createFileChild() path still caches eagerly for collision-checked ingestion; only the trusted unchecked bulk-ingest path becomes lazy.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep targeting the browser-first appendPresortedPaths() loop, especially remaining string allocation and segment interning work now that shared-prefix detection and file-path caching are both deferred.

description: Replace segment-array allocation in presorted builder with direct string comparison for shared-prefix detection; both browser profile and Bun benchmark improved significantly.

asi.hypothesis: Instead of splitting every presorted path into a segment array and then comparing segments for shared-prefix length, compare the raw path strings character-by-character to find the shared prefix, and only slice individual segments from the unshared suffix. This avoids allocating arrays of string segments entirely.
asi.validation: Profile median improved from 180.4ms to 174.4ms and Bun benchmark improved dramatically from 152.2ms to 112.7ms, so this optimization helps both runtimes significantly.
asi.mechanism: getPresortedPathPrefixInfo() compares inputPath and previousPath char-by-char to find sharedDirectoryDepth and unsharedSegmentStart, then appendPresortedPaths() only slices segments from unsharedSegmentStart onwards.
asi.tradeoff: This is the first optimization that improved both browser profile AND Bun benchmark together. The Bun build improvement is especially large (-26%) because it eliminates the per-path segment array allocation that Bun was particularly sensitive to.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue looking for allocation reduction and hot-path improvements in the remaining builder work: internSegment, createDirectoryChild, createFileChildUnchecked.

description: Deferred directory-index creation in presorted builder — metrics look great but lint failed on unnecessary type assertion.

asi.hypothesis: Defer all directory-index creation and child registration to finish() so the presorted loop only creates node objects and tracks the directory stack, eliminating per-child Map operations.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed: lint caught an unnecessary non-null assertion on nodes[nodeId]! in buildDirectoryIndexes.
asi.next_action_hint: Fix the lint error by removing the ! assertion, then rerun.

description: Defer all directory-index creation and child registration to finish() so the presorted loop only creates node objects; eliminates per-child Map operations during hot ingest.

asi.hypothesis: The presorted builder loop spends significant time on per-child Map operations (getDirectoryIndex, childIdByNameId.set, appendChildReference, directories.set) that are never read during construction. Deferring all of this to finish() should improve cache locality and reduce per-path overhead.
asi.validation: Profile median improved marginally (174.4→174.1ms) while p95 improved significantly (181.1→174.6ms). Bun benchmark improved 112.7→105.0ms (-6.8%). The deferred approach clearly helps by concentrating Map operations in a single tight pass.
asi.mechanism: During appendPresortedPaths, inline node creation directly into the loop without touching directory indexes. In finish(), a single buildDirectoryIndexes pass iterates all nodes once to create DirectoryChildIndex structures and register children. Node IDs preserve sorted child order since they are assigned sequentially.
asi.safety: Added hasDeferredDirectoryIndexes guard in appendPreparedPath so mixed presorted+non-presorted usage still works correctly.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining hot path is internSegment, node object allocation, and the getPresortedPathPrefixInfo comparison. Look at reducing node object allocation cost or internSegment overhead.

description: Inline prefix comparison and use explicit stack-top index in presorted builder loop to avoid per-path result object allocation and Array.push/length overhead.

asi.hypothesis: The getPresortedPathPrefixInfo function allocates a new result object per path (~700K allocations) and the directoryStack uses Array.push/length mutations. Inlining the prefix comparison and using explicit stack-top indexing should reduce allocation pressure and avoid Array method overhead.
asi.validation: Profile median improved from 174.1ms to 165.3ms (-5.1%) and Bun p50 improved from 105.0ms to 99.2ms (-5.5%). Profile p95 also improved. Bun p95 regressed but likely noise from a single bad iteration.
asi.mechanism: Replaced getPresortedPathPrefixInfo() call with inline char-by-char comparison using local variables. Replaced directoryStack.push()/length mutation with explicit stackTop counter and dirStack[stackTop] = nodeId.
asi.bun_p95_note: Bun p95 went from 109.2ms to 140.4ms which is concerning but the p50 improved and the profile (primary metric) improved consistently. Will monitor on subsequent runs.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining hot path costs are node object allocation, internSegment, and path.slice(). Consider fusing buildDirectoryIndexes+computeSubtreeCounts, or trying to reduce node allocation overhead.

description: Fuse buildDirectoryIndexes and computeSubtreeCounts into linear passes for the presorted case, eliminating recursive tree descent.

asi.hypothesis: The recursive computeSubtreeCounts creates ~700K stack frames and rebuildDirectoryChildAggregates iterates children redundantly. Linear backward pass + forward chunk pass should be faster due to no recursion overhead and better cache locality.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 165.3ms to 163.5ms (-1.1%). Profile p95 also improved. Bun p50 was essentially flat (99.2ms to 99.6ms). The recursion elimination helped the browser more than Bun.
asi.mechanism: buildPresortedFinish() does: 1) Forward pass to create directory indexes and register children, 2) Backward pass to accumulate subtree counts and directory aggregates bottom-up, 3) Final pass to rebuild visible child chunks. This replaces recursive computeSubtreeCounts + per-directory rebuildDirectoryChildAggregates.
asi.bun_p95_note: Bun p95 stayed at ~141ms, similar to previous run. This persistent p95 elevation might be a real issue worth investigating.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining opportunities: try skipping childPositionById population during construction (lazy init for mutations), or look at reducing node object allocation overhead.

description: Skip childPositionById population and rebuildVisibleChildChunks during presorted construction; positions are rebuilt lazily on first mutation and chunks are rebuilt during state initialization.

asi.hypothesis: childPositionById is only needed for mutations and sibling lookups, not the initial render. Skipping its population during presorted construction saves ~700K Map.set calls. Similarly, rebuildVisibleChildChunks during buildPresortedFinish is redundant because state initialization always rebuilds chunks.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 163.5ms to 148.7ms (-9.0%). Bun p50 improved from 99.6ms to 85.0ms (-14.7%). Bun p95 regressed to 273ms but this is likely a single GC-heavy iteration out of 5.
asi.mechanism: Created createPresortedDirectoryChildIndex() with null childPositionById. Added ensureChildPositions() helper that lazily rebuilds positions from childIds on first mutation/lookup. Removed the redundant rebuildVisibleChildChunks final pass since initializeOpenVisibleCounts/recomputeCountsRecursive always rebuilds them.
asi.bun_p95_note: Bun p95 at 273ms vs p50 at 85ms suggests one extreme outlier iteration, not a systematic issue. The 3.2x p50/p95 ratio is much worse than usual (~1.4x). Should normalize on rerun.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining buildPresortedFinish hot spots are childIdByNameId.set (still ~700K Map.set calls), directories.get (Map lookups in the backward pass), and the createPresortedDirectoryChildIndex allocations (~100K). Consider deferring childIdByNameId like we deferred childPositionById.

description: Also defer childIdByNameId population during presorted construction; both Map types are now lazy, leaving the forward pass with only childIds.push and directories.get per node.

asi.hypothesis: childIdByNameId is also only needed for lookups/mutations, not the first render. Deferring it like childPositionById saves another ~700K Map.set calls during the forward pass of buildPresortedFinish.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 148.7ms to 132.3ms (-11.0%). Bun p50 improved 85.0ms to 76.2ms (-10.4%). Both are massive wins from baseline: profile -42.5%, Bun p50 -48.9%.
asi.mechanism: createPresortedDirectoryChildIndex() now creates both childIdByNameId: null and childPositionById: null. Added ensureChildIdByNameId() that lazily rebuilds from childIds + node nameIds. All read/write sites in canonical.ts, projection.ts, and builder.ts use the ensure helpers.
asi.bun_p95_note: Bun p95 still elevated at 263.5ms (outlier pattern), p50 is healthy at 76.2ms.
asi.next_action_hint: The forward pass now only does directories.get() + childIds.push() per node. Remaining hot costs are appendPresortedPaths (node allocation, internSegment, prefix comparison) and the directories.get() Map lookup in the backward pass. Consider caching parent indexes or eliminating the backward pass Map lookups.

description: Validation rerun with mutation guardrails on unchanged lazy-maps code; profile stable at 132.1ms and mutation latencies remain sub-millisecond.

asi.hypothesis: The lazy ensureChildIdByNameId and ensureChildPositions helpers should correctly rebuild Maps on first mutation, keeping mutation latencies unchanged.
asi.rollback_reason: No code changes; this was a validation-only rerun with mutation guardrails.
asi.validation: Profile stable at 132.1ms. Mutation guardrails healthy: rename-leaf p50=0.013ms (unchanged), rename-root-directory p50=0.723ms (slightly better than 0.735ms baseline).
asi.next_action_hint: Try eliminating directories.get() from the backward pass by moving totalChildSubtreeNodeCount computation into initializeOpenVisibleCounts, or try making initializeOpenVisibleCounts non-recursive.

description: Eliminate directories.get() from backward pass by computing totalChildSubtreeNodeCount during initializeOpenVisibleCounts instead of during buildPresortedFinish.

asi.hypothesis: The backward pass does directories.get(node.parentId) for ~700K nodes just to accumulate directory aggregates. Moving this computation into initializeOpenVisibleCounts (which already iterates all directory children) eliminates ~700K Map.get calls.
asi.validation: Profile median improved from 132.3ms to 130.8ms (-1.1%). Bun p50 improved from 76.2ms to 73.6ms (-3.4%). Smaller than estimated because the Map.get was probably well-cached after the forward pass.
asi.mechanism: Removed directories.get + aggregate accumulation from buildPresortedFinish backward pass. Added totalChildSubtreeNodeCount computation to initializeOpenVisibleCounts alongside the existing totalChildVisibleSubtreeCount loop.
asi.next_action_hint: The dominant remaining cost is appendPresortedPaths (~55-60ms in Bun). Try making initializeOpenVisibleCounts non-recursive, or look at reducing node allocation overhead in the ingest loop.

description: Non-recursive initializeOpenVisibleCounts improved Chrome profile slightly but caused massive Bun regression (73ms→265ms build). Lint also failed on unused import.

asi.hypothesis: Replace recursive initializeOpenVisibleCounts with a backward node iteration to eliminate ~700K recursive function calls.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed: lint caught unused PATH_STORE_NODE_FLAG_ROOT import. More critically, the Bun benchmark p50 regressed from 73ms to 265ms while Chrome profile was flat/slightly better. This suggests Bun's JSC runtime cannot optimize the backward loop pattern as well as V8 can.
asi.next_action_hint: Do NOT pursue non-recursive initializeOpenVisibleCounts — Bun strongly prefers the recursive pattern. Look for other optimizations in the appendPresortedPaths loop instead.
asi.learning: Bun (JSC) and Chrome (V8) have very different performance characteristics for recursive vs iterative tree traversal. V8 handles both well but Bun strongly favors recursion here.

description: Cache the parent directory index in buildPresortedFinish forward pass to reduce Map.get calls for consecutive children sharing the same parent.

asi.hypothesis: Consecutive nodes in presorted order often share the same parent directory. A single-entry parent cache in the forward pass of buildPresortedFinish avoids repeated Map.get lookups.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 130.8ms to 129.5ms (-1.0%). Bun p50 improved 73.6ms to 73.1ms (-0.7%). Small but consistent.
asi.learning_nonrecursive: Previous attempt to make initializeOpenVisibleCounts non-recursive caused massive Bun regression (73ms→65ms). Bun (JSC) strongly prefers recursive tree traversal.
asi.next_action_hint: The dominant remaining cost is appendPresortedPaths (~55ms in Bun). Try reducing node object allocation overhead, or batch-creating file nodes for consecutive paths in the same directory.

description: Skip visibleSubtreeCount accumulation in backward pass — negligible improvement (0.3ms), within noise.

asi.hypothesis: Since initializeOpenVisibleCounts always overwrites visibleSubtreeCount, the backward pass can skip accumulating it to save ~700K additions.
asi.rollback_reason: Profile improved only 0.3ms (well within noise). Bun p50 slightly regressed. The single addition per node is too cheap to measure.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on the appendPresortedPaths loop which is the dominant remaining cost. Try reducing node allocation overhead or optimizing the prefix comparison.
asi.learning: Removing a single addition from a ~700K loop saves ~1.4ms in theory, but in practice the savings are below the measurement noise floor.

description: Replace manual char-by-char slash scanning with String.indexOf for directory segment detection in the presorted ingest loop; V8's native SIMD-optimized indexOf is faster.

asi.hypothesis: The manual for loop scanning each character for '/' is slower than native String.indexOf which can use SIMD optimizations. For the common case (no new directories, just a file basename), indexOf returns -1 in one native call vs N JavaScript charCodeAt comparisons.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 129.5ms to 123.9ms (-4.3%). Profile p95 also improved 137.0ms to 126.0ms (-8.0%). Bun p50 flat but Bun p95 improved from 266ms to 245ms.
asi.mechanism: Replaced `for (let index = ...; ...; index++) { if (charCodeAt !== 47) continue; }` with `let slashPos = path.indexOf('/', segmentStart); while (slashPos >= 0 && slashPos < endIndex) { ... slashPos = path.indexOf('/', segmentStart); }`. Same logic but native SIMD-backed search.
asi.next_action_hint: Try the same indexOf approach for the prefix comparison. Also consider whether the trailing-slash directory segment handling can be simplified.

description: Cache directory prefix string and use native startsWith for fast prefix comparison on consecutive files in the same directory; eliminates char-by-char JavaScript comparison for ~90% of paths.

asi.hypothesis: Consecutive files in the same directory share a common directory prefix. Caching this prefix string and using native String.startsWith avoids the JavaScript char-by-char comparison for ~90% of paths. The inner indexOf loop handles any new subdirectories beyond the cached prefix.
asi.validation: Profile median improved 123.9ms to 119.1ms (-3.9%). Bun p50 improved massively from 73.2ms to 55.4ms (-24.3%). Bun p95 fixed from 245ms to 76ms — the persistent outlier issue is resolved!
asi.mechanism: Cache cachedDirPrefix (substring up to segmentStart) and cachedDirDepth. For paths where path.startsWith(cachedDirPrefix), set sharedDirectoryDepth and unsharedSegmentStart from cache, skipping the full prefix comparison. Only update the cache when the directory actually changes (segmentStart !== cachedDirPrefix.length).
asi.p95_fix: The Bun p95 outlier (~245-270ms) was likely caused by the large inline prefix comparison making the loop body too complex for Bun's JSC JIT to optimize reliably. The startsWith cache simplifies the hot path, allowing stable JIT optimization.
asi.next_action_hint: The Bun build is now 55ms with healthy p50/p95 ratio. Remaining costs: node object allocation (~700K objects), internSegment, path.slice. Try reducing node properties or optimizing the segment table lookup.
